title: 2025/11/12 - SSC (Part 1)
displayTitle: 2025-11-12 - Social Services and Community Committee - 2024/25 Annual Review of the Ministry for Pacific Peoples [Part 1]
topic: 2024/25 Annual Review of the Ministry for Pacific Peoples
description: Wednesday, 12 November 2025 | Social Services and Community Committee 2024/25 Annual Review of the Ministry for Pacific Peoples Hearing of evidence: Ministry for Ethnic Communities Gerardine Clifford-Lidstone, Secretary for Peoples Bernadette Scanlan, Deputy Secretary, Policy and Insights Danilo Coelho de Almeida, Deputy Secretary, Corporate and Support Services Florence Malama, Acting Deputy Secretary, Commissioning and Partnerships James Greenwell, Director, Data and Insights Ripine Tito, Private Secretary to the Minister for Pacific Peoples
descriptionSummary: 2024/25 Annual Review of the Ministry for Pacific Peoples. Ministry for Ethnic Communities. Gerardine Clifford-Lidstone, Secretary for Peoples

Oh, good morning and welcome to the slick money. Uh, so we have you, so just before 9:00 PM uh, sorry, 9:00 AM I should say. Uh, we're going to need to move into the house, but, uh, look, thank you for being here with us this morning. We want to make some open comments to introduce yourselves and, uh, then move to questions from members. Yes. Thank you. Um, I just want to take a moment to acknowledge the National Day of Reflection being observed across New Zealand today.

This day invites us to pause, to remember, and to reflect and to recognize the responsibility we all carry to help shape the future where we truly care about safety, dignity, and respect. This year, we've had the wonderful benefit of, um, the, a survivor, Dr.

Jenny Hohepa. Tupu, contributing to our work through our advisory group, and the stories encourage of survivors. Remind us why the work we do matters. Because every policy, program, or partnership sit in front of people, families, and communities whose trust we must continue to honor and uphold.

In 24, 25, we stayed close to our communities, listened deeply, acted with purpose. We remain grounded in our values, energized by the people we serve. I want to sincerely thank the Pacific communities across New Zealand for their trust. Uh, your voices and aspirations guide everything that we do.

And I also want to thank our staff for their dedication, creativity, and serving community. We've developed use to continue, uh, setting a path, values and focus on education, business development, employment, housing, health, and languages. And before I hand back to you, I'd like to introduce my team at the ministry.

So we've got Danielo, Danielo, Alda, um, Florence Malama, and Bernadette Scanlon. We're a very small team and we work closely together, so they will actively, uh, contribute. They jump in at times to help with our Tylenol today. Um, m back to you.

Thank you very much, um, coming. Hey, thank you so much for, um, the report and all of the work that you've undertaken. I've got, um, lots of different questions. It's hard because I know I'm not gonna be able to ask them all. Uh, so I think where, where I'm going to start is probably with staffing levels.

Um, I want you just to talk through the decline that we've seen already and then also to, um, let us know if you're anticipating that to further decline in the next year. Um, and I know that in the past, Geraldine, you've said that you've been able to kind of consolidate the resource that you, uh, have got despite the stick declining in staff.

But clearly there's got to be some impact on your work program. Uh, and I want to know where you've had, where you've experienced a reduction or you've had to pull back on areas because you have had the staff reductions that you have. Yeah. Um, I'd like to start by saying that we are a small agency with a really big mission at the moment.

Our staffing levels are sit at 99. Um, FTE we undertook a really comprehensive process, uh, in the previous two years to, as you mentioned, uh, cluster and consolidate, uh, the functions that we do. We have had, uh, some turnover this year, which, uh, we've been, I I I've been really delighted about.

'cause what it means is that staff have had the opportunity to grow, develop, and move into other roles around the public service. In terms of our core functions, we have delivered on everything that we undertook to deliver. Um, I haven't seen any, you know, there, there will always be comments from as we more, um, the, about some of those interventions, perhaps No, around, uh, the, the future Staffing.

Yeah. One of those, um, strategic priorities that we've delivered on is our Pacific Healthy Homes Initiative. And so it's about tackling the, um, root causes contributing to poor Pacific Health. So, um, um, cold d unsafe housing. Over the past year, we've delivered over 10,000 housing home intervention.

So these are things such as insulation, vent, ventilation, safety, um, you know, one family in elderly, couple in their seventies with three mouna in their care, one with a chronic health illness repeatedly hospitalized. So we helped to upgrade their, um, home through in implementation of ventilation, insulation, um, and basic safety repair, such as fixing broken windows.

And so that resulted in a warmer, drier, healthier home. And more importantly, for the mouna, um, less hospital visits. At the heart of it, it's about, um, intergenerational health gains, but also reduced health costs to the system. So that's just one of 489 households that we've been able to create warmer, healthy, uh, dry homes.

Um, we don't, we haven't seen, uh, um, in terms of, uh, we, we staff out, we, we don't anticipate that there will be any changes or reductions going forward. You're Not anticipating a further decline in staffing numbers going into the next Financial year. I, I think the, the main thing is we are thinking ahead, but we don't, we, as much as possible, we try and preserve the interaction that our staff have with communities.

Um, and so if we were to look at anything like that, um, it would be in the context of what's happening in the environment, the and all opportunities data, and we haven't got end, but he's looking to, uh, be more efficient, do better always with the, the community need, um, at heart.

And I also add, you've mentioned, um, yes, we might have reduced our numbers over time, but we also like to think of it, we've got really focused team. I'm really proud that our policy and insights team have increased the number of their advice from 126 pieces to 200 over the course of the year. So we've increased our output whilst having a really dedicated team.

Okay. So I've just got two more subs on this. So, um, there has been an expectation across government agencies and MPP has not been precluded from this, that you cut back on, uh, back room functions, I guess. And so is as part of the reason why you're still struggling with that and still, there's still a need for improvement in that area because you do not have the backroom function and backroom support that you need. We have, uh, really developed our organizational maturity and as we, um, have considered what the organization needs, there is a level of expertise around government systems that has now been introduced to the ministry.

We've made some amazing improvements over the period of that three years. And remember that the first two years was, um, under extreme change of conditions. So we've made a lot of improvements, we're heading in the right direction. And, um, always reminded that continuous improvement is a really important part of good stewardship of the organization.

I mean, uh, I know the ratings from the last two or three audits reports remained the same, um, around the area you just, we had last, last year in there were issued on the accrued. Yeah. Um, my final question is just in relation to the priority that MPP has set around, uh, economic development for Pacific peoples. Uh, you also have had a, um, significant decline in funding for your business related program. Particularly in light of the fact that we've got a 12.1% unemployment rate for Pacific peoples and Pacific businesses are struggling alongside so many at the moment in al. Uh, we continue to work really closely with the agencies that have responsibility, both unemployment and economic development.

Um, we invest just under $10 million in economic growth. Uh, last year we saw 484 jobs created through the Pacific Business Village, and 778 businesses supported as well as 149 jobs created under the OLA Business Fund. So, um, I think that where we've made savings, again, has been in that back office space.

We are still putting funds into the economic growth, um, aspect of our work, and we are still seeing, um, improvements in our return. So these are, uh, above, uh, performance, uh, above the, um, performance metric that we had. I say a supplement coming, sort some of the answers actually.

Um, and I just wanna acknowledge this, the, the increasing reports that you mentioned, uh, which is, you know, a significant increase, but, um, just scanning the horizon, you mentioned digital and technology. Tiana, uh, contributions so far?

I'm, I'm just really enthused by, uh, the engagement that we can have with some of these bigger agencies and the openness to, um, our expertise in helping to synthesize the data, the data that they have. Um, yeah, I don't know if there's anything we wanted.

Um, we, one of the things that we are doing, and I'm, I'm actually very proud of our staff for their ability to work with agencies. Good example of what we're talking about is our work with, um, uh, ministry of Health. Um, so other agencies will be contributing data for the census.

We're working with agencies like the Ministry of Health to improve the quality of their collection, analysis and interpretation so that, um, we can be more prepared for the next census.
